background_section
## Background "I belong to this place called Igyagig in Yupik, which means 'where Lake Iliamna is swallowed by the Queijek river,' which then flows into Bristol Bay, Alaska." For millennia, Yup’ik, Dena’ina, and related communities have lived with **Nila Vena** (Lake Iliamna)—**Nila** in Dena’ina, **nunbukbuk** in Yup’ik. Six federally recognized tribes—Newhalen, Iliamna, New Halen, Pedro Bay, Kokhanok, and Igyagig—maintain that relationship today. America’s [seventh-largest lake](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject) by area, it runs [about 90 miles](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject) end to end and anchors a [global sockeye stronghold](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject). Tributaries sustain multiple distinct sockeye lineages, major bird migrations, and the planet’s last freshwater seals; the system also draws anglers and conservation scrutiny. For many villages, the lake is **the** drinking-water source. Since **ANCSA (1971)**, leaders describe a persistent squeeze: title and authority splintered; village life tied more tightly to fixed parcels while corporations hold large blocks. Tribes remain federally recognized yet exercise limited control over lands and waters relative to state-chartered Native corporations. They sit between **~8 million acres** of national park (Lake Clark, Katmai) and intense mineral interest—including what would be North America’s largest open pit—plus scattered claims staked after ANCSA. The organising imperative is dual: defend a globally significant salmon nursery and rebuild Indigenous authority over fragmented estates. ### Origins and Network The Igyagig-scale team links the **Native Corporation**, **tribal councils** and stewardship offices, **Alaska Venture Fund** (sponsor of the Nila Vena Sustainability Forum), a **health consortium**, and **Bristol Bay Heritage Land Trust**—actors already accustomed to joint, multi-million-dollar federal packages. The shift now is toward an **Indigenous land trust** pathway that re-centers stewardship in tribal hands.
Sources

Iliamna, AK, United States
place
Lake Iliamna, Alaska, USA
activities
Nila Vena Sustainability Forum|Nila Indigenous Land Trust Strategy
description
A tribal-led initiative focused on protecting the Nila Vena (Lake Iliamna) region through Indigenous land trusts, cultural revitalization, and economic regeneration in partnership with health and conservation institutions.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Organisers draw on **92+ combined years** of board service across partner bodies. The land trust is framed as repair for ANCSA’s fractures: stronger Native authority over land and water, salmon systems protected, and culture sustained. The theory of change is familiar governance plus strategic acquisitions and easements—parcel by parcel, table by table—toward durable tribal control in a landscape occupied for more than **8,000 years**.
bioregional_context
The Lake Iliamna region in Alaska is home to Yup'ik, Dena'ina, and Alutiiq peoples and some of the world's last wild sockeye salmon runs. The area is ecologically vital and under threat from industrial mining claims, despite being surrounded by national parks and protected ecosystems.
category
Bioregional Organizer
